query: comparison of noise levels between high-flow nasal cannula and bubble CPAP in preterm infants

retrieved_docs.title: Bubble CPAP in Infants -- Equipment -- Nasal interface to deliver PEEP
retrieved_docs.content: Long tubing nasal cannula (eg, RAM cannula, Neotech, Valencia) is another alternative modality for administering bCPAP. Long-tubing nasal cannulas are easy to apply and well-tolerated by neonates. Still, it has been associated with unreliable pressure delivery, decreased transmission of the oscillatory pressure, large air leaks, and increased need for CPAP pressure. [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Bubble CPAP in Infants -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Bubble CPAP in Infants -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Briefly, bCPAP can be differentiated from other types of CPAPs by its key feature, pressure oscillations (hence the name "bubble" CPAP), which promote airway opening and enhance lung recruitment. [9] Multiple bCPAP studies have shown to lower oxygen requirements, decrease respiratory decompensation, reduce the need for mechanical ventilation, reduce the incidence of chronic lung disease, and decrease the length of NICU stays. [10] This improved efficacy, ease of setup, and minimal equipment needs are reasons for its increased uptake in the NICUs, especially in low- to middle-income countries. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Bubble CPAP in Infants -- Indications -- Apnea of Prematurity
retrieved_docs.content: Preterm neonates are at risk for apnea, either from an immature central drive (central apnea), upper airway obstruction (obstructive apnea), or a mixture of both. Bubble CPAP has been shown to reduce apnea of prematurity; however, comparative studies with other treatment methods are lacking. [26] The mechanisms for such effects are poorly understood. [22] Bubble CPAP is believed to relieve obstructions in the extrathoracic airway by serving as a passive splint to maintain upper airway patency. It may also alter the Hering-Breuer inflation reflex, which allows the infants to adjust to increased respiratory work. [27] Further, authors have suggested that nasal CPAP reduces central apnea through constant nasopharyngeal stimulation and improved oxygenation by maintenance of FRC. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Bubble CPAP in Infants -- Clinical Significance -- Failure Rates
retrieved_docs.content: In a systematic review and meta-analysis, bCPAP was associated with lower failure rates when compared to other forms of CPAP in preterm neonates (RR 0.75; 95% CI was 0.57 to 0.98). [66] Another systematic review and meta-analysis also noted a similar finding, comparing bCPAP to ventilator CPAP. [11] In another study, bCPAP was associated with a shorter duration of noninvasive support in preterm infants less than 30 weeks gestation compared to infant flow devices. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Treatment / Management -- Assisted Ventilation of the Neonate
retrieved_docs.content: Non-invasive Respiratory Support: Nasal Intermittent Positive Pressure Ventilation (NIPPV) appears superior to CPAP alone for decreasing extubation failure, the need for intubation in preterm infants, but the same in cost and safety. [28] The primary difference in NIPPV and CPAP is that NIPPV requires a ventilator to provide positive pressure ventilation, while CPAP may use a less expensive device such as bubble CPAP to deliver the appropriate pressures.

retrieved_docs.title: Bubble CPAP in Infants -- Indications -- Postextubation Respiratory Support
retrieved_docs.content: Nasal CPAP, eg, bCPAP, has been used after extubation to reduce extubation failures and has also proven effective over other noninvasive respiratory methods. [21] [22] The use of bCPAP specifically resulted in reduced extubation failure rates in preterm infants born at younger than 30 weeks gestation who were initially intubated for less than 14 days in a subgroup analysis of a randomized clinical trial (RCT) that included 140 preterm infants. [23] This can be attributed to bCPAP's ability to maintain adequate lung volumes, cause airflow stimulation of the nasal passage and upper airway, and reduce obstructive apnea in preterm infants. [24] Additionally, the pressure oscillations specific to bCPAP likely contribute further to reduced reintubation rates. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Bubble CPAP in Infants -- Equipment -- Nasal interface to deliver PEEP
retrieved_docs.content: Various options are available for the bCPAP interface, including short binasal prongs, nasopharyngeal tubes, nasal masks, and nasal cannulae. [22] A Cochrane review from 2008 found that short binasal prongs are the preferred interface to administer bCPAP as they have the lowest resistance to the flow of gas and improvement in respiratory parameters when compared to single nasal prongs and nasopharyngeal prongs. [37] The same review found that the rates of extubation failure within 7 days of extubation were significantly lower when extubated to short binasal prongs. Despite the advantages, binasal prongs are associated with increased nasal injuries compared to other interfaces. [38]

retrieved_docs.title: Bubble CPAP in Infants -- Technique or Treatment -- Initiation and Titration
retrieved_docs.content: If the preterm infant develops apneas, especially if recurrent, a different mode of noninvasive or invasive ventilation is often recommended after addressing mechanical problems like airway obstruction from secretions. Clinicians must also explore the underlying reasons for recurrent apneas before escalating support.

retrieved_docs.title: Bubble CPAP in Infants -- Technique or Treatment -- Weaning and Discontinuation
retrieved_docs.content: But, consensus guidelines for an optimal strategy to wean off bCPAP are lacking. [56] Several weaning methods exist—gradual titration of CPAP (ie, PEEP) and then off respiratory support; gradual transition to other modes of support like nasal cannula (high or low flow); increasing time off CPAP; or sudden withdrawal of CPAP to room air. [57] RCTs and meta-analyses suggest that extremely preterm neonates can be successfully weaned off CPAP by 32 to 33 weeks postmenstrual age and 1600 g of body weight. [54] [55] Similarly, RCTs report preterm neonates, especially those born at less than 28 weeks gestation, have better success when gradually weaned off CPAP when compared to sudden weaning. [55] [58] [59] On the other hand, for a term infant, weaning from CPAP to no respiratory support can also be considered. No matter the strategy, close monitoring of FiO 2 , work of breathing, and airway patency is required while weaning. [60]

retrieved_docs.title: Bubble CPAP in Infants -- Clinical Significance -- Cost and Use Benefits
retrieved_docs.content: Bubble CPAP has the advantage of being cost-effective compared to other forms of CPAP. [43] [82] At its simplest setup, bCPAP requires an air compressor, oxygen blender, humidifier, tubing, and pressure generator, now available at a fraction of the cost of mechanical ventilation. [41] Current commercially available bCPAP can be relatively affordable at 15% of the cost of a mechanical ventilator. [43] Thus, bCPAP can be an economical way to provide respiratory support. Besides the cost-effectiveness, nurses can safely start and maintain bCPAP independently after minimal training, showing widespread applicability, especially in resource-limited settings. [43]

retrieved_docs.title: Bubble CPAP in Infants -- Equipment -- Nasal interface to deliver PEEP
retrieved_docs.content: The nasopharyngeal tubes are prongs that end at the nasopharyngeal level and have been perceived as challenging to use and poorly tolerated. It has the potential of being prone to kink, being easily blocked by secretions, and being proven to be inferior to other interfaces. [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Bubble CPAP in Infants -- Equipment -- Pressure generator liquid
retrieved_docs.content: Sterile water is utilized to generate PEEP.

retrieved_docs.title: Bubble CPAP in Infants -- Technique or Treatment -- Initiation and Titration
retrieved_docs.content: When CPAP is used for postextubation support in extremely preterm neonates, a higher PEEP of 9 to 11 cm H 2 0 is more effective in avoiding extubation failure. [51]

retrieved_docs.title: Bubble CPAP in Infants -- Equipment -- Circuit for Continuous Flow
retrieved_docs.content: For premature newborns who are particularly at high risk of oxygen toxicity and retinopathy of prematurity, WHO recommends blended O 2 with a fraction of inspired oxygen (FiO 2 ) of 0.3 or room air (FiO 2 of 0.21). [4] Moreover, without an O 2 blender, regulating FiO 2 and maintaining target oxygen saturation is challenging. [36]

retrieved_docs.title: Bubble CPAP in Infants -- Clinical Significance -- Effect on Distending Pressure
retrieved_docs.content: The bCPAP system confers a constant distending pressure, which is advantageous over using a humidified high-flow nasal cannula (HHFNC), where the amount of distending pressure varies and is difficult to measure. [8] [88] Moreover, a loss of distending pressure caused by malfunctions, like a large leak around the nasal prongs or dislodgement of the nasal prongs, can be easily identified by the disappearance of bubbling, a unique feature of bCPAP.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1723
retrieved_docs.content: The term خديج designates infants who have completed 29–30 weeks of gestation, and this developmental stage is directly linked to the occurrence of متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة (pulmonary immaturity syndrome). Consequently, babies classified as خديج are at risk for respiratory complications arising from lung immaturity, highlighting the importance of recognizing this gestational age category when assessing and managing neonatal respiratory health.

retrieved_docs.content: delivery and include the narrower method of delivery by fetal suction. Operations to facilitate delivery are defined as types of cesarean delivery, reinforcing the central role of surgical birth techniques. Together, these interconnected entities and relationships provide a comprehensive framework for understanding the clinical, procedural, and data‑driven aspects of labor, delivery, and related obstetric conditions, supporting informed decision‑making and optimized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2927
retrieved_docs.content: Other procedures inducing or assisting delivery are classified as a subset of other procedures to assist delivery, indicating that these specific interventions are encompassed within the broader category of techniques used to facilitate and support the childbirth process.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2402
retrieved_docs.content: Delivery of vertex presentation refers to a vaginal delivery of the fetus when the baby is positioned head‑first, representing a specific type of vaginal delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 4 -- Community 2849
retrieved_docs.content: Cesarean section is identified as a specific type of the Arabic term “الولادة القيصرية” (cesarean birth). The condition described as INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT is clinically associated with “الولادة القيصرية”, indicating that cesarean deliveries are linked to the occurrence of preterm infants with low birth weight. This connection underscores the need for careful obstetric decision‑making and targeted neonatal care when a cesarean birth is performed.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2387
retrieved_docs.content: Other procedures inducing or assisting delivery is a broad obstetric category that encompasses the specific outcome of فشل التوليد بالملقط (forceps delivery failure). Within this framework, الولادة بالملقط (forceps delivery) is directly linked to فشل التوليد بالملقط, indicating that the failure represents a particular adverse event or subcategory of the forceps delivery procedure. Consequently, both الولادة بالملقط and its associated failure, فشل التوليد بالملقط, are situated under the larger grouping of other procedures that induce or assist delivery, highlighting their interrelated roles in assisted vaginal birth and the importance of recognizing potential complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2443
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are collectively classified as an extremely premature birth of unspecified weight.
